---
title: "model_input"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
library(dplyr)

base_dir <- "/projectnb/dietzelab/arober/pecan/modules/prob.tools"
code_dir <- file.path(base_dir, "R")
fwd_dir <- file.path(code_dir, "forward_model")

source(file.path(code_dir, "utils.r"))
source(file.path(code_dir, "types.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "broadcast_rules.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "model_input.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_list.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_table.r"))
source(file.path(fwd_dir, "ensemble_input_broadcast.r"))
```


# Representing a Single Model Input
The `ModelInput` class encodes the set of inputs required to run a model.
It is a container with *input slots* ("slots" for short) and *metadata*, 
both of which are named lists with well-specified orders. Input slots
are different types or pieces of the input, making up the bits that comprise
the full set of inputs required to run a model. The metadata is structured the
same as the inputs, but is reserved for side information that does not 
constitute a true model input.

Under the hood, a `ModelInput` is actually a tree-like data structure; the
leaves are the tree consist of the union of all input slots and metadata.
Input slots and metadata are allowed to be any R object (vectors, arrays,
lists, etc.).

## Input Slot

The `InputSlot` class is a light wrapper around an R object used to tag that
object as an input slot.

```{r}
x <- InputSlot(4)
y <- InputSlot(list(1,2, list(3,4)))
z <- InputSlot(array(1:12, dim=c(2,3,2)))

print(x)
cat("\n")
print(y)
cat("\n")
print(z)
```

The raw value of the object can be accessed via the `value` field.
```{r}
x$value
```

## Input Slot

The `MetadataSlot` class is structurally exactly the same as `InputSlot` but is
instead used to label an object as metadata.

```{r}
a <- MetadataSlot(1:4)
print(a)
```

## Constructing a ModelInput Object
A `ModelInput` is a tree in which all leaves of the tree are either of type 
`InputSlot` or `MetadataSlot`. A `ModelInput` object is typically instantiated
by providing a nested list to the `ModelInput` constructor. A nested list
is by itself an ambiguous representation of a tree, as it is unclear whether
a sub-list should represent a leaf or another branch of the tree. `ModelInput`
thus imposes the following rules:  
1. Any object of type `InputSlot` or `MetadataSlot` is a leaf.  
2. Any non-list object that is not tagged as one of these slots is assumed to 
be an `InputSlot` by default. This default can be overridden by passing
`untagged_is_slot = FALSE` to the constructor.

The following examples illustrate the consequences of these rules.

```{r}
x <- list(a=1, b=2, c=c(3,4), d=list(e=5,f=6, g=list(h=1)), i=matrix(1:4))
y <- list(a=MetadataSlot(1), b=2, c=c(3,4), d=InputSlot(list(e=5,f=6, g=list(h=1))), i=matrix(1:4))
z_list <- list(a=1, b=2, c=c(3,4), d=list(e=5,f=MetadataSlot(6), g=InputSlot(list(h=1))), i=matrix(1:4))

x <- ModelInput(x)
y <- ModelInput(y)
z <- ModelInput(z_list)
z_prime <- ModelInput(z_list, untagged_is_slot=FALSE)


cat("x:\n")
summary(x)

cat("\ny:\n")
summary(y)

cat("\nz:\n")
summary(z)

cat("\nz_prime:\n")
summary(z_prime)
```
All nodes in a `ModelInput` are required to be named, with unique names at each
level of the tree. The names are used to construct unique keys of the form
`node_lvl1/node_lvl2/node_lvl3` for each node. As shown above, the `summary`
method prints these keys for all leaves, differentiating between input slots
and metadata. Trying to produce a tree with duplicate names at the same level
will produce an error.

```{r}
x <- list(a=1, b=list(c=2, d=list(e=3, e=4)))
try(ModelInput(x))
```

A more detailed visualization of the tree can be produced with `print_tree()`,
which optionally prints the class of the leaf values.

```{r}
print_tree(z, include_leaf_class=TRUE)
```
## Flat List Representation

While the tree representation is useful for organizing input types, ultimately
only a flat (non-nested) list of input slots is required for interfacing with
the model runner APIs.

A flattened list containing all leaves can be obtained using `flatten_model_input()`.
The traversal order is always left-to-right, depth first.

```{r}
flatten_model_input(z)
```

Helper functions are also provided to contain flattened lists of only input
slots and metadata, respectively. By default, these function strip the 
`InputSlot` or `MetadataSlot` class attribute from the returned values, but
this default can be overwritten by passing `return_raw_values=FALSE`.

```{r}
cat("Reminder of input structure:\n")
summary(z)

cat("\nFlattened input slots:\n")
flatten_input_slots(z)

cat("\nFlattened metadata slots:\n")
flatten_metadata_slots(z)
```

The flat representation can be re-constructed by passing separate flattened
lists for the input slots and metadata, with the keys specified in the 
list names. Crucially, flattening and then unflattening preserves the order
the input slots and metadata slots within each of these groups. However, it 
does not necessarily preserve the overall order of the leaves. As the tree
nodes are all required to be named, it is generally recommended to rely on 
names instead of order. However, it may be important to preserve the input
slot order in certain applications.


```{r}
cat("\nOriginal model input:\n")
summary(z)
cat("\nwith leaf order:\n")
leaf_names(z)

flat_input_slots <- flatten_input_slots(z)
flat_metadata_slots <- flatten_metadata_slots(z)

z_new <- unflatten_model_input(flat_input_slots, flat_metadata_slots)

cat("\nRe-constructed model input:\n")
summary(z_new)
cat("\nwith leaf order:\n")
leaf_names(z_new)
```
